items.q: What does the 'I' in FITT stand for?
items.why: Students guess. FITT = Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type - the variables you adjust to apply progressive overload. Intensity = how hard (e.g., % of 1RM or heart rate).

items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands so the body keeps adapting.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible immediately.
items.why: Students think 'overload' means 'maximum'. It means slightly exceeding current capacity - too much causes injury; too little stalls progress. The key word is PROGRESSIVE.

items.correct: Volume of air inhaled or exhaled in one normal breath at rest.
items.misconception: The maximum air your lungs can hold.
items.random_wrong: Air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation.
items.why: Students confuse tidal volume (resting breath, ~0.5L) with vital capacity (max breath, ~4-5L). Different lung volumes serve different purposes - tidal is the normal small ebb and flow.

items.q: What does the R in RAMP stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Raise. “Run” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Static stretches and water. “Sprints” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
